Researchers at Winship Cancer Institute of Emory University and Children’s Healthcare of Atlanta led a new study on childhood cancer survivorship.

The study shows that childhood cancer survivors who engage in long-term survivorship programs and receive a personalized survivorship care plan (SCP) live longer than survivors who do not receive an SCP.
The study was published in the Journal of the National Cancer Institute.
The research provides the first evidence that survivorship care plans integrated into comprehensive pediatric survivorship programs are associated with significantly improved survival.

Sharon M. Castellino, MD, MSc, co-led the multidisciplinary study as a professor and pediatric oncologist at the Aflac Cancer and Blood Disorders Center of Children’s Healthcare of Atlanta and as a researcher at Winship Cancer Institute.

Xu Ji, PhD, MSPH, served as lead author and is a researcher at Winship Cancer Institute and an associate professor in the Department of Pediatrics at Emory University School of Medicine and the Aflac Cancer and Blood Disorders Center of Children’s Healthcare of Atlanta.

The research team followed 3,366 childhood and adolescent cancer survivors diagnosed between 2002 and 2016 who were eligible for a survivorship program at a comprehensive pediatric cancer center, tracking outcomes for up to 10 years.
Exactly 55.9% of the 3,366 patients studied received a survivorship care plan (SCP).
Survivors who received an SCP had a 38% lower risk of death over 10 years compared with survivors who did not receive an SCP.
Survivors who had multiple survivorship visits and SCP updates experienced greater survival benefits than those with fewer visits or no updates.
When cause of death was examined, SCP recipients were less likely to die from relapse, treatment complications, or external causes than survivors who never received a plan.
The study is the first large, contemporary, and diverse cohort study to link SCP receipt within a pediatric cancer survivorship program to improved survival.
The Centers for Disease Control and Prevention (CDC) issued a call for evidence that survivorship care plans are an important tool for patients transitioning out of cancer treatment.
Researchers found that SCP engagement was not uniform across demographic or clinical groups.
Older survivors, survivors identified as non-Hispanic Black, survivors with central nervous system tumors, and survivors who received surgery-only treatment were less likely to receive an SCP.

The research team plans to continue tracking survivors beyond 10 years, to examine quality of life alongside survival, and to explore web-based platforms to help survivors and clinicians access SCPs nationwide.

Cancer SurvivorLink is a web-based platform developed by Jordan Marchak, PhD, ABPP, professor and co-director of the Aflac Survivor Program at the Aflac Cancer and Blood Disorders Center of Children’s Healthcare of Atlanta and researcher at Winship Cancer Institute.

The multidisciplinary study team included researchers from Winship Cancer Institute, the Aflac Cancer and Blood Disorders Center of Children’s Healthcare of Atlanta, Emory University School of Medicine, and Emory University Rollins School of Public Health.
The research was supported in part by the Centers for Disease Control and Prevention under Award SIP 20-004 and Cooperative Agreement U48DP006377.
The research was supported in part by the National Cancer Institute under grant R50CA285492.
The research was supported in part by the National Institute on Minority Health and Health Disparities under grant K01MD018637.

Thymic Epithelial Cell Alterations and Defective Thymopoiesis Lead to Central and Peripheral Tolerance Perturbation in MHCII Deficiency.
Major Histocompatibility Complex (MHC) class II (MHCII) deficiency (MHCII-D) is a rare combined immunodeficiency due to mutations in genes regulating expression of MHCII molecules.
MHCII deficiency results in impaired cellular and humoral immune responses, leading to severe infections and autoimmunity.
Abnormal cross-talk with developing T cells due to the absence of MHCII expression likely leads to defects in thymic epithelial cells (TEC).
The contribution of TEC alterations to the pathogenesis of this primary immunodeficiency has not been well characterized to date, in particular in regard to immune dysregulation.
We have performed an in-depth cellular and molecular characterization of TEC in this disease.
We observed an overall perturbation of thymic structure and function in both MHCII−/−mice and patients.
Transcriptomic and proteomic profiling of murine TEC revealed several alterations.
Impairment of lymphostromal cross-talk in the thymus of MHCII−/−mice affects mTEC maturation and promiscuous gene expression and causes defects of central tolerance.
We observed peripheral tolerance impairment, likely due to defective Treg cell generation and/or function and B cell tolerance breakdown.

Overall, our findings reveal disease-specific TEC defects resulting in perturbation of central tolerance and limiting the potential benefits of hematopoietic stem cell transplantation in MHCII deficiency.

Brother Kamalananda and author Philip Goldberg discussed “Autobiography of a Yogi” with Princeton University’s Hindu Life Program in a video.

Princeton University’s Hindu Life Program held a virtual seminar titled “AUTOBIOGRAPHY OF A YOGI: ITS LIGHT AND LEGACY, 75 YEARS LATER.” that focused on the enduring popularity and influence of Autobiography of a Yogi.
The virtual seminar was streamed in December in honor of the 75th anniversary of Paramahansa Yogananda’s world‑renowned spiritual classic Autobiography of a Yogi.

The event was hosted by Vineet Chander, assistant dean of Princeton University’s Office of Religious Life, who interviewed Self‑Realization Fellowship monk Brother Kamalananda and author Philip Goldberg.
Philip Goldberg is the author of the book “The Life of Yogananda: The Story of the Yogi Who Became the First Modern Guru.”
Philip Goldberg recalled that he first read Autobiography of a Yogi about 50 years ago during a time of great tension and turmoil and that the teachings remain an important antidote today.

Brother Kamalananda asserted that the message of Autobiography of a Yogi can be summarized in the sentiment “Reform yourself and you will reform thousands,” which he attributed to Paramahansa Yogananda.
Brother Kamalananda explained that world peace must begin in each individual heart and cannot be achieved solely through peace conferences or political meetings.

Brother Kamalananda quoted Paramahansa Yogananda: “Utopia must spring in the private bosom before it can flower in civic virtue, inner reforms leading naturally to outer ones. A man who has reformed himself will reform thousands.”

Advaita Vedanta is rooted in the Upanishadic tradition and represents a nondual Indian philosophical framework.
The term Advaita translates to non-duality, indicating the indivisible unity of all existence.
Adi Shankaracharya extensively formulated the Advaita Vedanta school.
Brahman is the supreme reality in Advaita Vedanta, described as unchanging, eternal, formless, pure consciousness (cit), existence (sat), and bliss (ananda) collectively known as sat-cit-ananda.
Brahman is the substratum of all existence and is self-manifesting and self-illuminating, beyond categorization.
Advaita Vedanta asserts the identity of the individual self (Atman) with Brahman as articulated in the Upanishadic mahavakya Tat Tvam Asi (Thou art That).
Ignorance (avidya) is said to veil the true nature of the self, causing the perception of separation between Atman and Brahman.
Maya is the cosmic illusion that creates appearance of duality and multiplicity, veiling Brahman and projecting empirical reality of forms, distinctions, and relationships.
Through Maya, the unchanging Brahman appears as the dynamic, ever-changing phenomenal world.
Paramarthika (Absolute Reality) is the realm of Brahman, unchanging and beyond mind and space.
Vyavaharika (Empirical Reality) is the everyday world of perception subject to change and duality.
Prabhasika (Illusory Reality) refers to dreams or mirages that are subjective and transient illusions.
Moksha involves realizing the non-dual nature of Brahman through knowledge (jnana).
Practices such as meditation and the study of scriptures help transcend ignorance and reveal the unity of Atman and Brahman.
Consciousness (cit) is the essence of Brahman, self-luminous, eternal, and transcending subject-object distinctions.
Pure consciousness of Brahman is non-relational and undifferentiated, unlike phenomenal consciousness.
Witness Consciousness (Sakshin) refers to the witnessing consciousness within the individual that remains untouched by the mind-body complex.
Sakshin illuminates the empirical self (jiva) like a lamp illuminating objects without being affected by them.
The jiva (empirical self) is a reflection of Brahman, distorted by limiting adjuncts (upadhis) of the body, mind, and senses.
Self-inquiry and meditation can enable the jiva to transcend limitations and recognize its identity with Brahman.
Maya creates the illusion of duality and obscures the unity of Atman and Brahman.
Experience of individual agency, subject-object relations, and multiplicity are attributed to Maya’s influence.
Liberation (moksha) involves overcoming Maya through knowledge (jnana), meditation, and direct experiential realization (samadhi).
In realized state, the distinction between knower, knowing, and known dissolves, resulting in oneness with Brahman.
Advaita Vedanta contributes to philosophical depth by addressing fundamental metaphysical questions about existence and consciousness.
The Advaita perspective on consciousness has implications for modern psychology, particularly regarding non-dual awareness and higher states of consciousness achieved through meditative practices.
Advaita Vedanta has influenced spiritual movements and practices worldwide, offering insights into self-realization and the unity of all existence.

A study led by researchers at Winship Cancer Institute of Emory University and Children’s Healthcare of Atlanta shows that childhood cancer survivors who engage in long-term survivorship programs and receive a personalized survivorship care plan (SCP) live longer than those who do not.
The research regarding childhood cancer survivorship was published in the Journal of the National Cancer Institute.
Sharon M. Castellino, MD, MSc, is a professor and pediatric oncologist at the Aflac Cancer and Blood Disorders Center of Children’s Healthcare of Atlanta and a researcher at Winship Cancer Institute.
Sharon M. Castellino, MD, MSc, co-led the multidisciplinary study on childhood cancer survivorship.

The research team followed 3,366 childhood and adolescent cancer survivors diagnosed between 2002 and 2016.
The study tracked outcomes for childhood and adolescent cancer survivors for up to 10 years.
55.9% of the patients studied received a survivorship care plan (SCP).
A survivorship care plan (SCP) is a personalized document that summarizes diagnosis and treatment history and outlines recommended screenings, late-effect monitoring, and healthy-living guidance.
Survivors who received an SCP had a 38% lower risk of death over 10 years compared to survivors who did not receive an SCP.
SCP recipients were less likely to die from relapse, treatment complications, or external causes than survivors who never received a plan.

Advaita Vedanta is rooted in the Upanishadic tradition.
The term "Advaita" translates to "non-duality".
Advaita Vedanta emphasizes the indivisible unity of all existence.
Adi Shankaracharya formulated Advaita Vedanta extensively.
Advaita Vedanta posits that the ultimate reality Brahman and the individual self Atman are fundamentally identical.
Brahman is described as an unchanging, eternal, and formless reality underlying the universe.
Brahman is pure consciousness (cit), existence (sat), and bliss (ananda), collectively described as sat‑cit‑ananda.
Brahman is self‑manifesting, self‑illuminating, and beyond categorization.
Brahman is neither an object nor dependent on anything external for its reality.
Advaita Vedanta asserts the identity of the individual self Atman with Brahman, as expressed in the Upanishadic mahavakya "Tat Tvam Asi" ("Thou art That").
Advaita Vedanta attributes the perception of separation between Atman and Brahman to ignorance (avidya).
Maya is the cosmic illusion that creates the appearance of duality and multiplicity in the world.
Maya veils the true nature of Brahman and projects the empirical reality of forms, dissonances, and relationships.
Through Maya, the unchanging Brahman appears as the dynamic, ever‑changing phenomenal world.
Advaita Vedanta describes three levels of reality: Paramarthika (Absolute Reality), Vyavaharika (Empirical Reality), and Prabhasika (Illusory Reality).
Paramarthika is the realm of Brahman, unchanging and beyond time and space.
Vyavaharika is the everyday world of perception, subject to change and duality.
Prabhasika is the realm of dreams or mirages, which are subjective and transient.
Liberation (Moksha) involves realizing the non‑dual nature of Brahman through knowledge (jnana).
Practices such as meditation and the study of scriptures help transcend ignorance and reveal the unity of Atman and Brahman.
In Advaita Vedanta, consciousness (cit) is the essence of Brahman.
Consciousness is self‑luminous, eternal, and transcends subject‑object distinctions.
Pure consciousness of Brahman is non‑relational and undifferentiated, unlike phenomenal consciousness.
Witness consciousness (Sakshi) within the individual refers to the witnessing consciousness that remains untouched by the mind‑body complex.
Sakshi illuminates the empirical self (jiva) and its experiences like a lamp illuminating objects without being affected by them.
The jiva (empirical self) is a reflection of Brahman, distorted by the limiting adjuncts (upadhis) of the body, mind, and senses.
Through self‑inquiry and meditation, the jiva can transcend these limitations and recognize its identity with Brahman.
Maya creates the illusion of duality, obscuring the unity of Atman and Brahman.
The experience of individual agency, subject‑object relationships, and multiplicity is attributed to Maya’s influence.
Liberation involves overcoming Maya through knowledge (jnana), meditation, and direct experiential realization (samadhi).
In the state of realization, the distinction between knower, knowing, and known dissolves, and one becomes one with Brahman.
Advaita Vedanta provides a profound understanding of existence, consciousness, and the nature of reality.

A new study led by researchers at Winship Cancer Institute of Emory University and Children’s Healthcare of Atlanta shows that childhood cancer survivors who engage in long-term survivorship programs and receive a personalized survivorship care plan (SCP) live longer than those who do not.
The research was published in the Journal of the National Cancer Institute.
The study provides the first evidence that survivorship care plans, when integrated into comprehensive pediatric survivorship programs, are associated with significantly improved survival.

The multidisciplinary study was co-led by Sharon M. Castellino, MD, MSc, professor and pediatric oncologist at the Aflac Cancer and Blood Disorders Center of Children’s Healthcare of Atlanta and researcher at Winship Cancer Institute.

Lead author Xu Ji, PhD, MSPH, is a researcher at Winship Cancer Institute and associate professor in the Department of Pediatrics at Emory University School of Medicine and the Aflac Cancer and Blood Disorders Center of Children’s Healthcare of Atlanta.
The research team followed 3,366 childhood and adolescent cancer survivors diagnosed between 2002 and 2016 who were eligible for a survivorship program at a comprehensive pediatric cancer center.
The survivors were tracked for outcomes for up to 10 years.
Just over half of patients studied (55.9%) received a survivorship care plan (SCP).
Those who received an SCP had a 38% lower risk of death over 10 years compared with those who did not.
Survivors with multiple survivorship visits and SCP updates experienced even greater survival benefits.
SCP recipients were less likely to die from relapse, treatment complications, or external causes than survivors who never received a plan.
Previous studies had not shown a direct link between SCPs and survival.
This research is the first large, contemporary, and diverse cohort study to link SCP receipt within a pediatric cancer survivorship program to improved survival.
Researchers found that SCP engagement was not uniform across demographic or clinical groups.
Survivors who were older, identified as non-Hispanic Black, or had central nervous system tumors or surgery-only treatment were less likely to receive an SCP.
The team plans to continue tracking survivors beyond 10 years, examine quality of life alongside survival, and explore web-based platforms to help survivors and clinicians access SCPs nationwide.

One such platform is Cancer SurvivorLink, developed by coauthor Jordan Marchak, PhD, ABPP, professor and co-director of the Aflac Survivor Program at the Aflac Cancer and Blood Disorders Center of Children’s Healthcare of Atlanta and researcher at Winship Cancer Institute.

The multidisciplinary study team included researchers from Winship Cancer Institute, the Aflac Cancer and Blood Disorders Center of Children’s Healthcare of Atlanta, and Emory University’s School of Medicine and Rollins School of Public Health.

This work was supported in part by the Centers for Disease Control and Prevention (Award SIP 20-004; Cooperative Agreement U48DP006377), the National Cancer Institute (R50CA285492) and the National Institute on Minority Health and Health Disparities (K01MD018637).

Major Histocompatibility Complex (MHC) class II (MHCII) deficiency (MHCII-D), also known as Bare Lymphocyte Syndrome (BLS), is a rare combined immunodeficiency caused by mutations in genes regulating MHCII molecule expression.
MHCII deficiency results in impaired cellular and humoral immune responses, leading to severe infections and autoimmunity.
The absence of MHCII expression leads to abnormal cross-talk with developing T cells, which likely causes defects in thymic epithelial cells (TEC).
MHCII−/− mice and patients with MHCII deficiency exhibit an overall perturbation of thymic structure and function.
Transcriptomic and proteomic profiling of murine thymic epithelial cells (TEC) in MHCII−/− mice revealed several alterations.
Impairment of lymphostromal cross-talk in the thymus of MHCII−/− mice affects medullary thymic epithelial cell (mTEC) maturation and promiscuous gene expression.
Impairment of lymphostromal cross-talk in the thymus of MHCII−/− mice causes defects of central tolerance.
MHCII deficiency is associated with peripheral tolerance impairment, likely due to defective Treg cell generation and/or function and B cell tolerance breakdown.
Disease-specific thymic epithelial cell (TEC) defects in MHCII deficiency result in the perturbation of central tolerance.
Thymic epithelial cell (TEC) defects in MHCII deficiency may limit the potential benefits of hematopoietic stem cell transplantation.

A study led by researchers at Winship Cancer Institute of Emory University and Children’s Healthcare of Atlanta shows that childhood cancer survivors who engage in long-term survivorship programs and receive a personalized survivorship care plan live longer than those who do not.
The research was published in the Journal of the National Cancer Institute.
The study provides the first evidence that survivorship care plans integrated into comprehensive pediatric survivorship programs are associated with significantly improved survival.

The multidisciplinary study was co-led by Sharon M. Castellino, MD, MSc, professor and pediatric oncologist at the Aflac Cancer and Blood Disorders Center of Children’s Healthcare of Atlanta and researcher at Winship Cancer Institute.

Xu Ji, PhD, MSPH, researcher at Winship Cancer Institute and associate professor in the Department of Pediatrics at Emory University School of Medicine and the Aflac Cancer and Blood Disorders Center of Children’s Healthcare of Atlanta, is the lead author.
The study followed 3,366 childhood and adolescent cancer survivors diagnosed between 2002 and 2016 who were eligible for a survivorship program at a comprehensive pediatric cancer center.
Outcomes were tracked for up to 10 years.
55.9% of the patients studied received a survivorship care plan (SCP).
Survivors who received an SCP had a 38% lower risk of death over 10 years compared with those who did not.
Survivors with multiple survivorship visits and SCP updates experienced greater survival benefits.
When cause of death was examined, SCP recipients were less likely to die from relapse, treatment complications, or external causes than survivors who never received a plan.
This study is described as the first large, contemporary, and diverse cohort study to link SCP receipt within a pediatric cancer survivorship program to improved survival.
The study emphasizes that SCPs can save lives when embedded in comprehensive survivorship care.
The CDC called for evidence that survivorship care plans are an important tool for patients as they transition out of cancer treatment.
In childhood survivors, survivorship care is particularly important due to risk for late effects of treatment; quality care after transition can lead to decades of good health.

SCP engagement was not uniform across demographic or clinical groups; older survivors, those identified as non-Hispanic Black, or those with central nervous system tumors or surgery-only treatment were less likely to receive an SCP.
The authors plan to continue tracking survivors beyond 10 years and examine quality of life alongside survival.
The team plans to explore web-based platforms to help survivors and clinicians access SCPs nationwide, including Cancer SurvivorLink.
